wrong_belief: Sejjil is liquid-fuel like Shahab.

wrong_belief: Centre can dismiss state govts freely on political rhetoric.
correct_belief: Article 356 requires President's satisfaction on constitutional breakdown, not mere allegations.

wrong_belief: Rally obstructions violate only state poll code.
correct_belief: Fall under ECI's Model Code of Conduct enforced nationally.

correct_belief: Airport congestion signals governance failure in long-term capacity planning and Centre-state coordination under aviation policy frameworks.

subject: Governance|Environment|Water Security
wrong_belief: Jal Jeevan Mission covers both rural and urban water supply equally.
correct_belief: JJM is rural-focused; urban water supply falls under AMRUT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation).

subject: Governance|Environment|Water Security
wrong_belief: JJM's success is measured by number of tap connections installed.
correct_belief: JJM's success requires functional, continuous, quality water supply—Sujal Gaon ID tracks these outcome metrics, not just installation numbers.

subject: S&T|Security|Space Technology
wrong_belief: NavIC is a global positioning system like GPS, providing worldwide coverage.
correct_belief: NavIC is a regional system limited to India and 1500 km radius; it is not a global alternative to GPS.

subject: S&T|Security|Space Technology
wrong_belief: NavIC failures are purely technical issues unrelated to India's strategic autonomy.
correct_belief: NavIC reliability directly impacts India's strategic independence from US GPS dependency and Atmanirbhar Bharat commitments in space technology.

subject: Security|Defense Manufacturing|S&T
wrong_belief: Pinaka MBRL standard range is 120 km.
correct_belief: Enhanced Pinaka variant operational range is approximately 75 km; older specifications cited 120 km but current operational system differs.

subject: Security|Defense Manufacturing|S&T
wrong_belief: Pinaka operationalization is purely a military capability story with no governance implications.
correct_belief: Pinaka's full operationalization reflects India's Defence Acquisition Procedure, Atmanirbhar Bharat policy implementation, and defense industrial ecosystem maturity.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: Section 301 was enacted as part of the Uruguay Round (1994) trade negotiations.
correct_belief: Section 301 was enacted in the US Trade Act of 1974; it predates the Uruguay Round by 20 years.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: Section 301 investigations are WTO-compliant dispute resolution mechanisms.
correct_belief: Section 301 enables unilateral US action outside WTO frameworks; it is a unilateral trade remedy tool, not a multilateral dispute mechanism.

subject: IR|Security|Non-Proliferation
wrong_belief: Sejjil is a liquid-fuel ballistic missile like the Shahab series.
correct_belief: Sejjil is a solid-fuel ballistic missile, enabling quicker launch and higher operational readiness compared to liquid-fuel systems.

subject: IR|Security|Non-Proliferation
wrong_belief: Iran's missile program is purely defensive and does not violate international agreements.
correct_belief: Iran's ballistic missile program includes offensive capabilities that violate UNSCR 2231 range and payload restrictions.

subject: IR|Security|Economy
wrong_belief: The US operation was a failure because it did not achieve regime change.
correct_belief: The operation's stated goal was to degrade Iran's military capability and oil infrastructure, not regime change. The miscalculation was underestimating Iran's resilience and regional retaliation speed—not the operation's design itself.

subject: IR|Security|Economy
wrong_belief: The Strait of Hormuz crisis is purely a regional Middle East problem with limited global impact.
correct_belief: The Strait of Hormuz is a critical global chokepoint through which ~20% of world oil passes. Disruption directly impacts global energy prices, supply chains, and inflation—affecting India's economy, import costs, and fiscal stability.

subject: Economy|IR|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: Section 301 probes are WTO-compliant dispute mechanisms.

correct_belief: India explicitly named among 16 economies for solar/steel/textiles scrutiny due to trade surplus.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: LPG supply crisis means India will run out of cooking gas; government failure is purely operational.
correct_belief: The crisis is primarily a price/cost shock transmitted through global markets, not necessarily a physical shortage. Supply disruption and price inflation are distinct policy challenges requiring different responses (emergency reserves vs. subsidy management).

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: Force majeure in energy contracts means suppliers can suspend obligations indefinitely without penalty.
correct_belief: Force majeure suspends performance obligations only while the event makes performance impossible; suppliers retain duty to mitigate losses and resume supply once conditions normalize. Contracts may include price adjustment clauses.

subject: IR|Security|Energy
wrong_belief: IDF's denial of regime change means the operation is limited and low-risk for escalation.
correct_belief: Denying regime change as a goal does not preclude escalation. The operation's scale (700+ targets) and remaining targets suggest potential for further strikes. Escalation risk depends on Iranian retaliation, not just Israeli intentions.

subject: IR|Governance|Ethics
wrong_belief: India's constitutional prohibition of forced labor (Article 23) automatically ensures compliance with international forced labor standards.
correct_belief: Constitutional prohibition is necessary but insufficient; effective enforcement requires supply chain monitoring, import verification systems, and international cooperation—which the US alleges India lacks.

subject: IR|Governance|Ethics
wrong_belief: Forced labor enforcement is purely a domestic labor law issue with no trade policy implications.
correct_belief: The US uses Section 301 to link forced labor enforcement to trade access, making labor standards a trade enforcement tool with direct tariff consequences for India's exports.

correct_belief: Post-deal signatories like Malaysia/India still face new Section 301 investigations, as probes launched March 11-12 despite prior agreements.

correct_belief: IEEPA invalidated but Section 301/122 remain viable (slower, sector-specific); Section 301 predates WTO via 1974 Trade Act.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: Section 301 investigations are WTO-compliant dispute resolution mechanisms similar to multilateral trade negotiations.
correct_belief: Section 301 enables unilateral US tariffs and trade actions outside WTO frameworks. It is a unilateral enforcement tool predating the WTO by 20 years (enacted 1974), not a multilateral dispute mechanism.

subject: IR|Governance|Ethics|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: India's constitutional prohibition of forced labor (Article 23) automatically ensures compliance with US forced labor standards and import verification requirements.
correct_belief: Constitutional prohibition is necessary but insufficient. Effective enforcement requires supply chain monitoring, import verification systems, third-party audits, and international cooperation—which the US alleges India lacks.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: Signing US trade deals guarantees immunity from Section 301 probes and tariff actions.
correct_belief: Bilateral trade agreements do not suspend or exempt countries from Section 301 investigations. India and Malaysia face new probes despite recent trade agreements, demonstrating that Section 301 operates independently of bilateral deals.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy
wrong_belief: IEEPA invalidation ends all US tariff powers, including Section 301.
correct_belief: IEEPA invalidation removes emergency tariff authority but leaves Section 301 (Trade Act 1974) and Section 122 intact. These are slower, sector-specific tools but more legally durable and harder to challenge.

subject: IR|Economy|Trade Policy|Ethics
wrong_belief: India's Article 23 ban on forced labor ensures full US compliance.
correct_belief: Article 23 prohibits forced labor domestically but requires supply chain audits and import verification for US standards.

correct_belief: The Strait of Hormuz is a critical global energy chokepoint through which ~20% of world oil passes. Disruption directly impacts global energy prices, supply chains, and inflation—affecting India's oil/LPG imports, fiscal stability, inflation management, and external sector balance.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: India's current account deficit widening is purely a trade deficit issue unrelated to monetary policy or RBI's policy space.
correct_belief: CAD widening (1.3% of GDP in Q3 FY26, expected to worsen) constrains RBI's monetary policy flexibility. Wider CAD signals external fragility, rupee depreciation pressure, and reduced forex buffers—limiting RBI's ability to cut rates aggressively even if growth slows, because rate cuts weaken the rupee further and worsen import costs.

subject: Economy|IR
wrong_belief: Slowdown purely domestic demand failure like consumption collapse.
correct_belief: Driven by external supply-cost shocks with resilient demand; year-on-year positive[1].

wrong_belief: Inflation stable as crude impact already priced in.
correct_belief: Retail inflation edging up from food; full crude passthrough pending, poses upside risk[1].

correct_belief: Upholds formula, adds only review mechanism for cess shortfalls.

correct_belief: Distinct reusable horizontal tech for rapid, low-cost launches.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: The slowdown is driven by weak domestic demand, similar to a cyclical recession requiring stimulus spending.
correct_belief: The slowdown is supply-driven (cost shocks, logistics disruption) with resilient demand (vehicle registrations +19.1% YoY, digital payments strong). Year-on-year trends remain positive; the issue is sequential moderation from external cost pressures, not demand collapse.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: India's current account deficit widening is purely a trade deficit issue unrelated to monetary policy or RBI's policy space.
correct_belief: CAD widening (1.3% of GDP in Q3 FY26, expected to worsen) constrains RBI's monetary policy flexibility. Wider CAD signals external fragility, rupee depreciation pressure, and reduced forex buffers—limiting RBI's ability to cut rates aggressively even if growth slows, because rate cuts weaken the rupee further and worsen import costs.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: India's March 2026 slowdown is primarily a demand-side crisis caused by consumption collapse, requiring broad fiscal stimulus and rate cuts.
correct_belief: The slowdown is supply-driven (cost shocks, logistics disruptions) with resilient domestic demand. Policy must target cost pressures and supply constraints, not stimulate demand. Broad rate cuts risk rupee depreciation and worsen import costs.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: Retail inflation is stable and under control; crude oil price increases have already been fully reflected in domestic prices.
correct_belief: Retail inflation is edging up from food prices, and the full impact of crude oil increases has not yet been reflected. This poses upside inflation risk if global energy prices remain elevated, constraining RBI's monetary policy flexibility.

subject: Economy|IR|Energy Security
wrong_belief: Economic slowdown is demand-driven collapse requiring broad fiscal stimulus and RBI rate cuts.
correct_belief: Supply-cost shocks (Hormuz/energy) with resilient demand (vehicle sales/digital payments up); stimulus risks rupee depreciation and import inflation.
